meta.note: Aggregated purely from abstracted patterns, not verbatim exam content. All 11 available years (2558-2568) are now analyzed: 2558-2561 via native pdftotext, 2562-2568 (scanned image PDFs with no text layer) via direct multimodal vision reading of PyMuPDF-rendered pages, since no OCR binary was available in this environment. Re-aggregate by merging knowledge/analyzed/<year>.json whenever a new year's exam is added.
meta.exam_year_naming_convention: Each file/label year (e.g. 2562) is the Thai academic year assessed; the exam is physically sat in Jan/Feb/Mar of year+1 (e.g. the '2562' paper's cover page is dated 1 Feb 2563). This is the official NIETS convention and is consistent across every year checked.

format_evolution: 2558-2563: 40 items. Standalone/reading items first (~18-22), then a long block of short 2-line dialogue completions (~18-22 items) filling out the rest.
2564-2566: format shrinks to 32 items, same two-part shape (standalone/reading first, dialogue completion block second), no explicit 'PART' labels printed.

2567-2568: 32 items, but the paper now explicitly prints 'PART 1: Language Use' (functional dialogue + phonics/intonation/punctuation, ~17-21 items) and 'PART 2: Reading' (signs/notices/charts/passages, ~11-15 items). Dialogue completion is no longer a single trailing block; short functional-dialogue items are interleaved throughout Part 1, and a growing share of them are 'reverse completion' (the question is missing, not the reply) rather than the traditional 'reply is missing' format.

sections.content: phonics, punctuation/intonation, functional language (incl. reverse-question items in 2567-2568), signs/notices, culture/holidays, vocabulary, data (chart/table/graph), map/directions, arithmetic and multi-constraint word problems, riddles (2567+), spatial-preposition items (2567+), short reading passage (poster/letter/diary/narrative/cross-cultural-comparison) with linked questions

sections.type: dialogue completion (older years) or interleaved functional dialogue (2567+)
sections.content: short 2-line dialogue completions across varied settings; increasingly includes reverse-completion and pragmatic-inference ('what does X mean/decide') items in the most recent years

exam_structure.answer_format: single correct choice, index 1-4 in source (use 0-3 zero-indexed in generated JSON)

vocabulary_topics_frequency.public_health_pandemic_era: medium (2563-2565 only; vaccines, masks, distance learning)
vocabulary_topics_frequency.cross_cultural_comparison: low-medium (2567+; Thanksgiving/Black Friday/food-sharing customs contrasted between countries)

question_type_catalog: phonics/vowel-sound odd-one-out (4 words, pick the one with different sound)
punctuation-mark correctness check (identify the incorrect OR the correct punctuation among 4 sentences; colon/semicolon/comma nuance in later years)
intonation-pattern odd-one-out (identify the question with different intonation, typically 3 wh- + 1 yes/no or vice versa)
situational response selection (given a scenario, choose what to say)
reverse dialogue completion (given the reply, choose the question that was asked) — increasingly common from 2567 onward
sign-meaning multiple choice (interpret a public sign/notice, incl. discriminating near-identical icons)
picture-based description, true-statement, or purpose-inference (choose true sentence / appropriate reply / why for a depicted scene)
culture/holiday knowledge multiple choice, including ambiguous-image cases where the generic answer is correct
data set with 2-5 linked questions (bar chart, pictograph, pie chart, or comparison table), including superlative reads, multi-series charts, and transitive-comparative-chain construction
map-based direction question (single or multi-turn, ordinal turn-counting, named streets, "You Are Here" markers)
arithmetic and multi-constraint word problems (totals, change, fractions of a group; budget + required-purchase + day-restriction rules in the newest years)

short reading passage (poster/letter/diary/narrative/cross-cultural-comparison, ~50-150 words) with 2-6 linked comprehension questions covering: main idea, specific detail, inference, vocabulary-in-context, sequence/timeline, paraphrase fidelity, reason ("why"), NOT-mentioned/NOT-correct, and TRUE/FALSE synthesis across two contrasted cultures
short 2-line dialogue completion across varied everyday settings (school, shop, phone, restaurant, travel, family), including pragmatic "what does X mean/decide" inference items
cloze passage (short narrative, 4-5 numbered blanks) each with 4 thematically-related vocabulary choices
reading_patterns: informational poster/announcement/notice/coupon: event or offer name, date, time, activity steps, prize/condition/discount rule -> questions on date-format reading, location, categorization of listed items, duration, best-title selection, and who-qualifies-for-what

informal personal letter/postcard/diary: recount of an event, trip, or routine -> questions on sender/writer identity, timing, duration, sequence of past events, inferred unstated facts (origin, start time, family size via precise entity counting), and vocabulary-in-context

short narrative recount or aspirational story (school trip, exhibition, activity, a child's daily routine/goal): -> questions on who/where/why, character relationships, emotional reaction, and reason-inference requiring the specific stated cause (not a passage-adjacent but unsupported detail)

instructional/rule text (classroom rules, safety instructions, procedural/sequential picture instructions e.g. mask disposal): -> questions distinguishing the stated rule from its stated consequence, or requiring correct step order

comparative data table, chart, or transitive-comparative-chain passage (quantities, speeds, prices, animal facts): -> questions requiring cross-referencing 2+ data points, correct superlative/comparative direction, or chaining multiple comparative sentences into one ranking

cross-cultural comparison passage (2 short paragraphs contrasting the same named custom/event between two countries, e.g. Black Friday US vs UK, food-sharing Asia vs West): -> questions requiring checking a claim against both halves, since a statement may be true for one side only
fact/negation passage (a single surprising fact, e.g. an animal's biology): -> requires precise negation-matching and avoiding over-generalized restatements
dialogue_patterns: greeting/situation cue -> appropriate short reply (2-4 word to short phrase answers)
problem-sharing exchange (failed test, minor accident, feeling unwell, pandemic illness) -> sympathetic or advice-giving reply, matched to emotional valence
wh-question exchange testing correct question-word usage and matching answer format/unit (time, price, quantity, floor, distance in km not m)
yes/no question exchange testing correct auxiliary agreement and polarity-consistency in the reply
service-context exchange (shop, restaurant, post office, reception, bank) -> polite request/response with price, quantity, or service-register focus
family or classroom exchange -> reply completes a permission, request, offer, or excuse pattern
phone-call exchange -> reply follows telephone-etiquette conventions (asking to repeat, giving/confirming a name, unavailability)
reverse-completion exchange (2567+): a troubled/informative statement is given as the reply, and the student supplies the eliciting question
pragmatic-inference exchange: the correct choice is an implied consequence or decision (what will happen / what does X mean), not a literally-stated fact
exclusion-logic exchange: a stated category is ruled out ("only Thai food", "no sodas"), and the correct answer is the one option outside/inside that category
sequential/procedural exchange: steps must be produced or matched in the correct real-world order
proposal-plus-justification exchange (2568): an agreement phrase precedes the speaker's own elaboration of a suggestion's benefit
distractor_patterns: correct topic/vocabulary but wrong grammatical form (wrong tense, wrong question word order, wrong auxiliary, tense inconsistent with a stated time marker)
emotionally or socially mismatched reply (wrong register, wrong tone, or wrong emotional valence for the situation)
answer restates or echoes the question/prompt instead of responding to it
plausible-looking detail lifted from a different part of the passage/picture (wrong character, wrong location, wrong number, wrong entity count)
comparison-direction reversed in table/chart items (faster/slower, more/less, largest/smallest swapped); one broken link in an otherwise-correct transitive comparative chain
arithmetic or constraint-satisfaction distractor from a common slip (forgetting a fraction, off-by-one count, violating one hidden rule in a multi-constraint word problem)
literal/unrelated dictionary meaning or numeric-entity confusion used as a distractor instead of the passage-supported meaning (e.g. confusing a count of hearts with a count of animals)
near-miss grammatically-almost-correct sentence (single word order, preposition, or tense error) placed among 3 clearly wrong options
exclusion-logic trap: distractor belongs to the very category the prompt explicitly ruled out
over-generalized negation (extends a specific stated fact - e.g. one animal's biology - into a false universal claim)
fixed-date distractor for an event whose date is actually variable ("the fourth Thursday" restated as one fixed calendar date)
wrong reverse-completion question that would elicit a different but related answer
NOT-mentioned distractor that is topically plausible but never actually stated in the passage
universally-equating distractor in cross-cultural comparison items (claiming both countries mean the same thing when the passage contrasts them)
difficulty_trend: Difficulty has crept upward over time: 2558-2563 skew easier/medium (straightforward lookup, single-clue inference); 2564-2566 introduce more multi-step reading and pandemic-era vocabulary; 2567-2568 add reverse-question dialogue, pragmatic inference, riddles, transitive comparative chains, multi-constraint word problems, and cross-cultural TRUE/FALSE synthesis — meaningfully harder than the 2558-2563 baseline.
answer_logic_principles: the correct answer is always fully derivable from explicit or clearly-implied information in the stimulus (picture, sign, passage, table, or dialogue) — never requires outside/world knowledge beyond common Thai grade-6 general knowledge

reading/passage items typically require synthesizing 2 nearby clues (e.g., a stated action + a stated time) rather than a single direct lookup, especially for inference, vocabulary-in-context, and paraphrase-fidelity items

table/chart items require correct directionality of comparison (more/less, faster/slower) across at least two data points, and in transitive-comparative-chain items, correctly combining 2-3 stated relations into one ranking

dialogue items require matching both grammatical form (question type) and topical/tonal continuity with the surrounding lines; reverse-completion items require finding the unique question consistent with the given reply

distractors are constructed to be genuinely plausible on a surface read — same topic/vocabulary family as the correct answer, or a near-miss on exactly one constraint/link — so accuracy depends on precise reading, not vocabulary elimination alone
multi-constraint word problems (budget/menu, rule-based) require satisfying every stated rule simultaneously, not just getting the arithmetic total right
cross-cultural comparison passages require checking a claim against the correct one of two contrasted halves, since many statements are true for only one side
topic_frequency_ranking: everyday dialogue functional language (requests, sympathy, opinions, reverse-question inference)
cloze passage vocabulary (varies by theme each year: environment, food, occupations, health)
reading passages: letters, diaries, posters, short narratives, cross-cultural comparisons
festivals, holidays, and culture (Thai and Western)
shopping, money, arithmetic, and multi-constraint word problems in English
health and body (incl. pandemic-era public health, 2563-2565)
science facts and diagrams (animal facts, water cycle)
